episode_text:
day=27; meal=bite-sized pizza and broccoli; setting=Social with person on Right; time_of_day=evening;
preferences: microwave_time=2 min; occlusion_relevance=minimize right occlusion; robot_speed=medium; skewering_axis=perpendicular to major axis; web_interface_confirmation=no; transfer_mode=outside mouth transfer; outside_mouth_distance=medium; convey_robot_ready_for_initiating_transfer=LED; detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfer_feeding=button; detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfer_drinking=button; detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfer_wiping=button; convey_robot_ready_for_completing_transfer=LED; detect_user_completed_transfer_feeding=perception; detect_user_completed_transfer_drinking=button; detect_user_completed_transfer_wiping=button; retract_between_bites=yes; bite_dipping_preference=do not dip; wait_before_autocontinue_seconds=100 sec

microwave_time.user_tendencies: IF food is a cold-served item such as fruit or dessert AND any dining context AND any affective state THEN no microwave BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected no microwave for clearly cold meals across personal, social, TV, and work contexts. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree without a strong time-pressure cue or dip/sauce emphasis THEN 2 min BECAUSE this remains the best-supported personal default for standard warm meals. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm finger-food or nugget-style meal with dip available THEN microwave preference is mixed between 1 min, 2 min, and 3 min BECAUSE personal dipped finger-food meals have shown both lighter and hotter reheating, so no single nondefault override is stable. IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal, including the newly observed pizza-and-broccoli meal, THEN 2 min BECAUSE repeated right-social warm meals consistently align with standard reheating. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 2 min BECAUSE observed left-social warm meals align with standard reheating. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm snack meal THEN microwave preference is mixed between 1 min and 3 min, so 2 min remains the most stable front-TV summary BECAUSE the user does not consistently want lighter or heavier reheating in that context. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN no microwave BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal follows the stable cold-food rule. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm entree or warm finger-food meal THEN microwave preference is mixed between 2 min and 3 min, with 3 min the stronger right-TV warm-meal summary BECAUSE multiple right-TV warm meals support hotter reheating. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a light dessert/fruit or sweet snack-style meal with topping or sauce THEN 1 min BECAUSE the right-TV bananas-and-brownies meal shows the user may prefer lighter reheating for sweet TV meals. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN microwave preference is mixed between 2 min and 1 min, with 2 min still the more conservative summary BECAUSE one left-TV warm meal used standard reheating while a later left-TV dessert/fruit meal with chocolate sauce used lighter reheating. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 1 min BECAUSE the user selected lighter reheating for one left-work warm meal. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 3 min BECAUSE the user selected the hottest reheating level in that right-work meal. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN no microwave BECAUSE the front-work fruit meal reinforces the stable cold-food rule. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm finger-food or warm entree meal with dip available THEN 1 min BECAUSE repeated front-work warm meals used lighter reheating, indicating a stable preference for less heating while actively working in front.

occlusion_relevance.user_tendencies: IF dining context includes a person on the right OR a TV/device on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal, including the newly observed right-social pizza-and-broccoli meal, THEN minimize right occlusion BECAUSE repeated right-side social and right-TV warm meals support avoiding blockage of salient right-side targets. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal or a light sweet snack/dessert meal THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE right-TV light-meal episodes show the user does not consistently prioritize right-side visibility for lighter TV meals. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN minimize left occlusion BECAUSE observed left-social meals support left-side occlusion minimization when a social partner is on the left. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN minimize front occlusion BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected front-occlusion minimization when the salient target was directly ahead. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal or a warm finger-food/entree meal with dip available THEN minimize front occlusion BECAUSE both front-work meals prioritized keeping the front device visible. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE repeated left-TV meals did not request left-side occlusion minimization. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit meal THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE the user repeatedly declined occlusion-based adjustment in left-side work contexts. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint, including warm meals with dip available and cold fruit/dessert meals, THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE personal meals consistently show no need for occlusion-based adjustment without a salient social or media target.

robot_speed.user_tendencies: IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint, including cold fruit/dessert meals and warm nugget-style meals with dip, THEN medium BECAUSE personal meals repeatedly support a comfortable moderate pace as the stable default. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm meal with dip or sauce available THEN robot speed is mixed between medium and fast, with medium the stronger personal summary BECAUSE multiple personal dipped meals favor a comfortable pace more often than a fast one. IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal, including the newly observed pizza-and-broccoli meal, THEN medium BECAUSE repeated right-social warm meals consistently use a comfortable socially appropriate pace. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal or a typical warm entree THEN medium BECAUSE observed left-social meals used the same moderate pace. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a light cold item or a warm snack/entree THEN speed preference is mixed between fast and medium, with newer front-TV evidence favoring medium, so medium is the more conservative front-TV summary BECAUSE the user does not always prioritize maximum speed when watching TV ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN medium BECAUSE right-TV warm and fruit meals repeatedly used medium speed. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a light dessert/fruit or sweet snack-style meal THEN fast BECAUSE the right-TV bananas-and-brownies meal showed a low-effort, media-focused pattern that prioritized efficiency for a light sweet meal. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN medium BECAUSE the earlier left-TV warm meal used a moderate pace. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a light dessert/fruit meal or sweet snack-style meal THEN fast BECAUSE the left-TV bananas-and-brownies meal used fast speed together with broad automation and no cues. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN fast BECAUSE the user selected fast speed while working, indicating that some task-focused meals prioritize efficiency. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed for fruit while working, suggesting the work-context preference for speed is moderated by meal type. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed in that right-side work context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN medium BECAUSE the front-work fruit meal aligns with the lighter-meal pattern of keeping a comfortable moderate pace. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm finger-food or warm entree meal with dip available THEN fast BECAUSE the front-work warm meal used fast speed, extending the task-focused efficiency pattern to front-work warm meals.

transfer_mode.user_tendencies: IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN outside mouth transfer BECAUSE this remains the conservative personal default, is physically feasible for the user, and is repeatedly supported in personal meals. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE personal light-meal evidence shows that for lighter cold meals the user can prefer lower-effort direct delivery even in a personal setting. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm meal with dip or sauce available THEN transfer preference is mixed between outside mouth transfer and inside mouth transfer, with inside mouth transfer slightly strengthened for dipped finger-food meals BECAUSE multiple personal dipped meals selected direct delivery, though outside-mouth transfer remains the broader personal default for non-dipped standard meals. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state suggests efficiency or lower-effort interaction OR food is a warm finger-food/snack-style item with dip available in an efficiency-oriented personal meal THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE personal finger-food meals have repeatedly paired direct delivery with lower-effort interaction choices. IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal, including the newly observed pizza-and-broccoli meal, THEN outside mouth transfer BECAUSE repeated right-social warm meals consistently favor the more conservative, user-controlled transfer style in that social context. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN outside mouth transfer BECAUSE the observed left-social warm meal shows that social context can favor the more conservative, user-controlled transfer style. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the earlier left-social light meal shows that in some social contexts the user prefers lower-effort direct delivery. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected direct delivery in front-TV contexts, indicating a stable preference to reduce the need to lean forward while attending to media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal or a light sweet snack-style meal THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE right-TV light-meal episodes align with a lower-effort media-focused strategy. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal THEN transfer preference is mixed between outside mouth transfer and inside mouth transfer, with inside mouth transfer slightly favored BECAUSE right-TV attention often pulls the user toward lower-effort direct delivery. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE repeated left-TV meals selected inside-mouth transfer. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit meal THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected direct delivery while working, showing that active device engagement favors reduced user movement. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user also selected direct delivery in that right-side work context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal or a warm finger-food/entree meal with dip available THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE front-work meals consistently favor lower-effort direct delivery when attention is on a device ahead.

outside_mouth_distance.user_tendencies: IF transfer mode is outside mouth transfer AND dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN medium BECAUSE the user often selected a moderate stopping distance that balances comfort and reachability across personal and social meals. IF transfer mode is outside mouth transfer AND dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm finger-food or bite-sized meal with sauce or dip available THEN outside-mouth distance is mixed between medium and far BECAUSE one personal fish-bites meal used a farther stop, showing that in some personal dipped finger-food meals the user prefers more space from the mouth. IF transfer mode is outside mouth transfer AND dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal, including the newly observed pizza-and-broccoli meal, THEN medium BECAUSE repeated right-social outside-mouth meals consistently used a moderate stopping distance. IF transfer mode is inside mouth transfer AND any dining context AND any affective state AND any food condition THEN not applicable BECAUSE this parameter only applies to outside mouth transfer.

convey_robot_ready_for_initiating_transf.user_tendencies: IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE this remains the conservative low-burden default outside more specialized personal meals. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN no cue BECAUSE personal light-meal evidence shows that for a light personal meal the user may prefer fully unobtrusive coordination. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm meal with sauce or dip available THEN cue preference is mixed between speech + LED and no cue, with speech + LED somewhat strengthened for personal dipped warm meals BECAUSE one personal dipped meal selected explicit multimodal cueing while another suppressed cues entirely. IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal, including the newly observed pizza-and-broccoli meal, THEN LED BECAUSE repeated right-social warm meals consistently support a quiet visual cue rather than no cue as the stable social-right pattern. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN LED BECAUSE the observed left-social warm meal shows the user can prefer a quiet visual-only cue in left-social settings. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the earlier left-social light meal shows the user may want a more explicit multimodal cue in some social settings. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN front-TV cue preference is mixed between no cue and LED, with repeated evidence favoring reduced interruption but later evidence showing acceptance of a quiet visual cue, so LED is the best-supported conservative summary BECAUSE it preserves low disruption without fully suppressing readiness information. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN LED BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal showed preference for a quiet visual-only cue. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal THEN speech + LED BECAUSE right-TV warm meals more strongly support explicit multimodal cueing than quiet cueing alone. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a light dessert/fruit or sweet snack-style meal THEN no cue BECAUSE the right-TV bananas-and-brownies meal shows that in a light media-focused meal the user can prefer fully unobtrusive coordination. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN cue preference is mixed between LED and no cue, with repeated left-TV evidence favoring no cue for media-focused low-effort meals. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN no cue BECAUSE the user selected no cue while working on a warm entree. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN speech BECAUSE the user selected speech in the same work context for fruit. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the user selected multimodal cueing in that right-side work context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN speech BECAUSE the front-work fruit meal showed preference for an audible cue. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm finger-food or warm entree meal with dip available THEN no cue BECAUSE the front-work warm meal used no cue, indicating that front-work cue preference depends on meal type and can shift toward minimal interruption for warm task-focused meals.

detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfe.user_tendencies: IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE explicit manual initiation remains the most conservative personal default when no stronger meal-specific factor is present. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN autocontinue BECAUSE personal light-meal evidence shows that for a light personal meal the user can prefer low-effort automatic feeding initiation. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm meal with sauce or dip available THEN feeding initiation is mixed between open mouth and autocontinue, with open mouth slightly strengthened for personal dipped warm meals BECAUSE one personal dipped meal selected open-mouth initiation while earlier personal dipped evidence favored lower-effort nonbutton initiation more generally. IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal, including the newly observed pizza-and-broccoli meal, THEN button BECAUSE repeated right-social warm meals consistently favor explicit manual initiation over lower-effort automation. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN button BECAUSE the observed left-social warm meal supports the explicit-control social pattern. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN open mouth BECAUSE the earlier left-social light meal shows a lower-effort social strategy can be preferred for a light meal. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN feeding readiness is mixed between autocontinue and button, with button the more cautious front-TV summary BECAUSE the user may want deliberate timing even while attending to media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN button BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal shows explicit manual initiation can be preferred in that media context for a light meal. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal THEN open mouth BECAUSE right-TV warm meals consistently favor low-effort perceptual initiation for feeding. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a light dessert/fruit or sweet snack-style meal THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the right-TV sweet-meal episode shows a stronger low-effort media-focused pattern than the right-TV fruit meal. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN feeding initiation is mixed between button and autocontinue, with newer left-TV evidence favoring autocontinue for media-focused low-effort meals. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the user selected automatic initiation while working. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation for feeding in that work-and-fruit context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation in that right-side work context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN open mouth BECAUSE the front-work fruit meal reinforced the work-plus-light-meal pattern of preferring low-effort perceptual feeding initiation. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm finger-food or warm entree meal with dip available THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the front-work warm meal used automatic feeding initiation, showing that front-work warm meals can favor stronger automation than front-work fruit meals.

IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE personal evidence supports manual readiness as the stable default. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN autocontinue BECAUSE personal light-meal evidence shows that for a light personal meal the user can prefer low-effort automatic drink initiation. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation for drinking in that right-side work context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN button BECAUSE the front-work fruit meal aligns with the left-work fruit case, reinforcing explicit manual sip initiation for light meals during work. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN front-TV completion cue preference is mixed between no cue and LED, with later evidence showing acceptance of a quiet visual cue, so LED is the best-supported conservative summary BECAUSE it preserves low interruption while still marking transfer completion. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN LED BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal showed preference for a quiet visual-only completion cue. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal THEN speech + LED BECAUSE right-TV warm meals more strongly support explicit multimodal completion cueing than quiet cueing alone. retract_between_bites.user_tendencies: IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree, a cold fruit/dessert meal, or a sauced/dipped personal meal THEN no BECAUSE personal evidence consistently favors skipping extra retract motions to keep the meal efficient. IF dining context includes a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal, including the newly observed pizza-and-broccoli meal, THEN yes BECAUSE repeated right-social meals strongly support retracting to reduce prolonged obstruction for the partner on the right. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN yes BECAUSE the observed left-social warm meal shows the user wants retraction in a left-social context when a partner is present during a standard warm meal. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN no BECAUSE the earlier left-social light meal shows the user does not always want extra retract motions in social settings, especially for a lighter meal. IF dining context includes a TV/device on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN yes BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal shows the user may accept longer meal time to reduce prolonged obstruction during a light media-focused meal. IF dining context includes a TV/device on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree, warm finger-food meal, or a light sweet snack/dessert meal THEN no BECAUSE repeated right-TV warm and sweet meals skipped retraction, showing that right-TV meals do not uniformly favor retracting and that warm right-TV meals more strongly support efficiency. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN preference is mixed, with both no and yes observed, so front-TV meals show context sensitivity rather than a single stable rule. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm finger-food or warm entree meal with dip available THEN no BECAUSE the front-work warm meal skipped retraction, showing that front-work retraction depends on meal type and is not uniformly driven by front-device visibility.

wait_before_autocontinue_seconds.user_tendencies: IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN 100 sec BECAUSE this remains the conservative personal default. IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm finger-food meal, including the newly observed pizza-and-broccoli meal, THEN 100 sec BECAUSE repeated right-social warm meals consistently support a moderate timeout rather than the shortest delay. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN wait preference is mixed between 1000 sec and 10 sec BECAUSE one earlier left-TV meal used a very long wait while a later left-TV dessert/fruit meal used the shortest timeout. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN 1000 sec BECAUSE the user selected a very long wait in that work-and-fruit context, showing that food/context combinations can reverse the earlier work-context tendency toward short waits. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN 1000 sec BECAUSE the front-work fruit meal matched the left-work fruit case, reinforcing that light work meals can favor a very long timeout.
